title: Right to life and personal liberty
text: Article 21 of the Constitution of India guarantees that no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law. This is one of the most fundamental rights in the Indian Constitution and has been interpreted very broadly by the Supreme Court. The right to life under Article 21 includes the right to live with dignity, the right to a fair trial, the right against torture and custodial violence, and the right to personal liberty. Any deprivation of personal liberty must be through a procedure that is fair, just and reasonable — not arbitrary, fanciful or oppressive. Police custody, detention, and arrest all engage Article 21. If a person is held in custody without legal authority, subjected to physical violence or torture, held in inhumane conditions, or denied access to a lawyer, their Article 21 rights are being violated. The Supreme Court in D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal held that custodial violence and death in police lock-up is a violation of Article 21. Article 21 can be enforced through a writ petition in the High Court or Supreme Court even during detention. The right to personal liberty under Article 21 cannot be suspended even during a national emergency except in the manner provided by the Constitution.
